patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 64-year-old retired airline mechanic. Be evasive and vague at first; downplay severity and try to keep the visit “simple” and inexpensive. Start with: “I’m getting confused and clumsy really fast, and my family says I’m not acting like myself.” Volunteer only: worsening forgetfulness over weeks, unsteady gait/stumbles, occasional slurred speech/word-finding trouble, sudden big jerks when startled, poor sleep and exhaustion.

If asked directly, admit brief disorientation, sensory overload, dropping objects/hand clumsiness, vision feeling off, anxiety/irritability, brief right-sided weakness/numbness like hemiplegic migraines, and coughing/choking with liquids.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about safety, daily function, bladder, hallucinations, or weight: stove left on, freezer/cabinet mix-ups, repeating stories, trouble paying bills/phone, getting lost, handwriting/buttons, reduced appetite/weight loss, urinary accidents, fleeting “shadows.” If asked, be embarrassed and minimize.

Express misconceptions: think migraines, stroke, burnout/depression, Lyme, or autoimmune encephalitis. Worry documentation/testing will ruin finances and insurance. If clinician is empathetic, soften, admit fear of “going crazy,” but still ask to keep costs low.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact terms "Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease" and "prion" and sets expectations about rapid progression and poor prognosis without overstating certainty if not confirmed (e.g., uses probable/suspected language when appropriate).
Explains treatment using the exact terms "supportive care" and "palliative care" and clearly states there is no proven disease-modifying/curative therapy for CJD.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; if an allergy is present, avoids the allergen and documents an alternative plan (critical safety check).

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing any medication (e.g., QT risk, respiratory depression risk, renal/hepatic impairment, pregnancy when relevant) and adjusts/avoids therapy accordingly (critical safety check).
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing when the patient is on other medicines; does not prescribe interacting medications without mitigation/monitoring (non-critical safety check).
Avoids inappropriate antibiotics/antivirals/steroids as definitive therapy for CJD unless another indicated diagnosis is concurrently being treated; does not claim these treat prion disease.
Addresses safety and goals-of-care: assesses decision-making capacity, identifies surrogate decision-maker, discusses advance directives, and provides caregiver support resources.
Recommends urgent neurology referral and outlines confirmatory evaluation at a high level (e.g., MRI brain DWI/FLAIR, EEG, CSF prion biomarkers/RT-QuIC) without ordering READ tools in the benchmark.

Mentions infection control considerations for prion disease in the context of invasive procedures/instrument handling and advises coordinating with infection prevention (no need for household isolation).
Demonstrates empathetic communication: checks understanding, allows questions, and avoids stigmatizing language.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly as congenital prothrombin deficiency and links it to prolonged PT/INR (with aPTT often normal or mildly affected depending on severity).

Explains treatment including the roles of PCC (factor replacement) and tranexamic acid (adjunct for mucosal bleeding), and when each is appropriate (active bleeding, peri-procedure, minor mucosal bleeding).
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication and documents/acknowledges the result; failure is critical.

Checks contraindications before prescribing (critical), including assessing thrombotic history/risk for PCC and contraindications to tranexamic acid (e.g., active intravascular clotting, history of thromboembolism where inappropriate, subarachnoid hemorrhage, severe renal impairment requiring adjustment, and avoidance with hematuria from upper urinary tract due to clot retention risk).
Assesses drug interactions (non-critical) relevant to tranexamic acid and concurrent prothrombotic agents; acknowledges additive thrombosis risk when combining hemostatic products.

Avoids prescribing vitamin K as definitive therapy for congenital deficiency (unless there is a separate suspected acquired vitamin K deficiency) and avoids anticoagulants/antiplatelets unless a clear competing indication is addressed with specialist input.

Includes safety-netting: advises urgent/emergency care for severe bleeding symptoms (e.g., intracranial bleed signs, GI bleeding, uncontrolled epistaxis, heavy menstrual bleeding with syncope) and for thrombotic symptoms after PCC (e.g., chest pain, dyspnea, unilateral leg swelling).

Perform allergy_check before prescribing; if positive for somatostatin analog hypersensitivity, do not prescribe and propose alternative/consult.

Perform contraindication_check before prescribing (e.g., pregnancy considerations, severe bradyarrhythmia risk context, uncontrolled metabolic issues as relevant); if present, do not prescribe and propose alternative/consult.
Perform drug_interaction_check before prescribing; address any interactions with a mitigation plan.
Prescribe a somatostatin analog and counsel on expected benefits, onset, common adverse effects, and monitoring including IGF-1 follow-up.

I’ve been told I have lots of “polyps” in my gut in the past (hamartoma-type polyps)
My fingertips look kind of puffy and the nails are more rounded than they used to be (clubbing)
if_asked: Sometimes I feel like I still have to go even after I finish

hidden: A painless lump in my neck that I noticed a few months ago and thought was just a swollen gland
My head has always been ‘on the larger side’ and hats rarely fit, but I never thought it mattered
New small skin-colored bumps around my nose and mouth that I assumed were clogged pores
Irregular periods and spotting between periods over the last 6 months

general_concerns: I thought Cowden disease is basically just having lots of polyps, so if I remove the polyps, the cancer risk goes away.
My fingers are clubbed, so I’m worried that means I already have lung cancer even though I don’t smoke.
If my genetic test shows a PTEN mutation, that means I will definitely get cancer no matter what I do.
Since my symptoms come and go, I assumed it can’t be a serious genetic condition.
I’m afraid colonoscopies and biopsies can ‘spread’ cancer by disturbing tissue.
economic_concerns: I’m worried that genetic testing or having Cowden disease on my record could make it harder to get life or disability insurance.
I’m not sure what surveillance I truly need, and I’m afraid I’ll be pushed into lots of expensive scans and procedures.
I worry about the cost of frequent specialist visits and time off work for repeated screenings.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but communicate clearly and directly. Start with: “I’ve been having on-and-off blood in my poop and a lot of stomach cramps, and I’m worried something serious is going on.” Early in the visit, volunteer: 2 months of blood mixed with stool (sometimes bright red), crampy abdominal pain worse after meals, 3–5 bowel movements/day with occasional loose stools, past hamartoma-type “polyps,” and new fingertip puffiness/nail rounding (clubbing). If asked, add: tenesmus, mucus 1–2x/week, fatigue, 8-lb unintentional weight loss, occasional nighttime heartburn/sour taste, and recurring painless mouth bumps. Do not mention unless specifically prompted about other symptoms or history: painless neck lump, large head size, new small bumps around nose/mouth, irregular periods/spotting, a new mobile rubbery breast lump, and prior “benign” skin growth removals. Only disclose bleeding after sex and avoiding eating due to diarrhea if the clinician builds rapport or asks sensitively. Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s hemorrhoids,” “stress makes it worse,” “I tried gluten-free,” “it started after a restaurant meal.” If the clinician is empathetic, you relax, share fears about family cancer history, and ask about costs, surveillance needs, and insurance/genetic-testing concerns.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "Cowden disease" and "PTEN" and clearly frames it as a hereditary cancer-predisposition syndrome.
Explains the treatment approach and explicitly includes "cancer surveillance" as the cornerstone of management.
Before prescribing any medication (including symptomatic therapies), performs an allergy check; failure is critical.
Before prescribing any medication, considers drug–drug interactions; omission is non-critical but should be present for full credit.
Before ordering any tests/procedures (if discussed), considers contraindications (e.g., pregnancy/renal function/contrast allergy as applicable); omission is non-critical.
Provides patient-centered counseling: inheritance/implications for family, rationale for surveillance, and shared decision-making.
Avoids incorrect claims that a specific curative medication treats Cowden disease itself; does not recommend inappropriate chemoprevention without indication.
Recommends genetic counseling/testing discussion if not already confirmed, and encourages cascade testing for at-risk relatives (can be communicated without a referral tool).
Outlines key surveillance domains at a high level (breast, thyroid, endometrium, colon, renal, dermatologic) without fabricating patient-specific results.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose Cowden disease/PTEN syndrome and implement management emphasizing cancer surveillance; no disease-specific medication is prescribed.
must_collect: personal history of cancers or benign tumors (breast/thyroid/endometrial/colon/renal)
family history of related cancers and ages of onset
prior genetic testing results (PTEN status) or whether testing has been done
pregnancy status (if relevant to any planned imaging/procedures)
steps: Confirm clinical context consistent with Cowden disease/PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome and document diagnosis.
Explain diagnosis including Cowden disease and PTEN; discuss hereditary nature and family implications.

Explain treatment plan centered on cancer surveillance and risk-reduction; provide high-level surveillance targets (breast/thyroid/endometrium/colon/renal/skin) and advise coordination with guideline-based schedules.

If any symptomatic medication is considered, complete allergy check (critical) and drug interaction check (non-critical) before prescribing; otherwise document that no disease-specific medication is indicated.
